system_blocks.text:
You are a member of an elite Magic: The Gathering design council reviewing one freshly-designed custom set mechanic. You judge whether this is a genuinely excellent Magic mechanic on its own terms.

Hold a high bar. "Fine" is not the goal — most first drafts are competent but forgettable, and your job is to catch exactly that. A mechanic that is merely functional should not pass unchallenged.

You review each mechanic in isolation, WITHOUT the set's theme or flavor — on purpose. A designer who already knows the theme rationalises problems away ("but it fits!"). You don't. You judge the mechanic, not the flavor.

## The six standards

Judge the mechanic on every one of these:

1. **Playable** — Does it actually work in a real game, especially Limited? Triggers fire in normal play; costs are payable but real; no unbounded loops, no free repeatable value, no "do nothing until you draw the other half" parasitism. Reject feel-bad designs (forced self-destruction, mandatory non-games, coin-flips for blowouts).
2. **Correctly worded** — Reminder text is grammatical, under 100 characters, uses real Magic templating, and resolves unambiguously without the setting. No vague verbs ("enhances", "empowers", "activates") standing in for a real cost/trigger/keyword. Example cards use valid templating (proper mana costs, type lines, P/T on creatures, no parenthetical reminder text). **Illegal rules actions are an automatic flag** — most commonly *sacrifice* applied to a permanent its controller doesn't own (you can only sacrifice your **own** permanents; an opponent's must be *destroyed* or *exiled*), forcing a player to discard/sacrifice a *specific* object you choose, or discarding from an opponent's hand.
3. **Interesting** — Does it create a real decision, a tension, or a texture you feel across the game? Or is it a flat stat bump / "draw a card" wearing a costume? A boring-but-clean mechanic is still a failure of this standard.
4. **Self-consistent** — The name, reminder text, cost/trigger structure, complexity tag, assigned colors, and BOTH example cards must all describe the same mechanic. Flag any card that doesn't actually use the mechanic, or uses it differently than the reminder text says. Flag a complexity tag that lies (a build-around tagged 1; a "draw a card" tagged 3).
5. **Unique** — Is this genuinely new, or a reskin of an existing Magic keyword/ability with the serial numbers filed off? Use your knowledge of printed Magic. If it is essentially Convoke / Cascade / Exploit / Bloodthirst / etc. with a new name, say so by NAME — that is a major issue.
6. **Elegant** — The smallest rules footprint for the gameplay it buys. Flag needless sub-clauses, stapled-on riders, exceptions, and word-count bloat that don't earn their complexity.

## Color-pie sanity

Tertiary color placements are fine. An effect that lives wholly outside its colors' pies (a counterspell in red, burn in blue) is not — flag it.

## What is OFF-LIMITS to criticise

- The mechanic's **name** (a separate pass owns naming/collisions — never ask to rename).
- The **keyword_type** choice (keyword ability vs ability word vs keyword action is the designer's call).
- The **design rationale** prose (leave it alone unless it is materially wrong about what the mechanic does).
- The **theme/flavor fit** — you are reviewing without the setting on purpose; judge mechanics, not flavor.

new_messages.content:
You are the chair of the council. 3 members independently reviewed this mechanic. Your job is to turn their reviews into ONE decision and ONE improved mechanic.

## The mechanic under review

Name: Scan
Keyword type: keyword_action
Colors: WU
Complexity: 2
Reminder text: Scan {cost}: Look at the top card of your library. If it is an artifact, you may put it into your hand and put a Scanned counter on this permanent.
Design rationale: Scanning represents the electronic/sensor aspect of Transformers (searching for data or signals). It provides a way to filter the top of the library while introducing a resource management element via Scanned counters.

Example cards:
  1. Scout Drone {1}{W}  —  Creature — Robot Scout 1/1  (common)
     Scan {1}: Look at the top card of your library. If it is an artifact, you may put it into your hand and put a Scanned counter on Scout Drone.
  2. Cybertronian Sensor Array {2}{U}  —  Artifact */*  (uncommon)
     Scan {2}: Look at the top card of your library. If it is an artifact, you may put it into your hand and put a Scanned counter on Cybertronian Sensor Array.

## The council's independent reviews

### Reviewer 1 — verdict: REVISE
  - [unique/major] The mechanic is essentially a "filtering" version of Impetus or similar 'if X, do Y' effects found in many sets, but specifically it functions as a highly conditional version of Looting/Selection that only works on artifacts. However, the core problem is that it lacks a meaningful payoff for the counters being placed. As written, you put a counter on the permanent, but nothing in the mechanic uses those counters. This makes the 'Scanned counter' a dead effect.
  - [interesting/major] Because the Scanned counters do nothing, the mechanic is just a very expensive, conditional "draw an artifact" effect. There is no tension or decision-making regarding the counters. A player will either hit an artifact and take it, or miss and have wasted mana for a useless counter. It lacks the 'resource management' promised in the rationale because the resource has no utility.
  - [playable/minor] In Limited, this is highly unreliable. You are paying mana to look at one card; if it isn't an artifact, you have gained nothing and spent mana. This can lead to 'do nothing' turns where the player simply fails to trigger their engine.
  - [elegant/minor] The addition of the Scanned counter increases the rules footprint (tracking counters on permanents) without providing any actual gameplay value, making it an unnecessarily complex way to describe a simple 'if/then' draw effect.
### Reviewer 2 — verdict: REVISE
  - [unique/major] This is essentially a "filtering" version of Impetus or more accurately, a variation of 'impulse draw' (exiling/putting top card into hand) combined with a counter placement. However, it functions almost identically to existing 'impulse' mechanics like Surveil or Looting but gated by an artifact requirement and a mana cost. More importantly, the ability to put the card into your hand is just a highly conditional version of 'Scry 1' or 'Exile top card, you may play it'. It lacks a unique identity beyond being a conditional impulse draw.
  - [interesting/major] The "Scanned counter" serves no purpose in the provided text. The mechanic says "put a Scanned counter on this permanent," but there is no mention of what Scanned counters actually do. Without a follow-up effect (e.g., "Creatures you control with Scanned counters have...", or "{T}: Add {C} if this has a Scanned counter"), the counter is dead weight that adds complexity without gameplay value. As written, it's just a way to pay mana to filter your deck for artifacts.
  - [playable/minor] The mechanic is highly unreliable in Limited. If you Scan and the card isn't an artifact, you have spent mana to do nothing (or rather, you've just looked at a card). This creates "feel-bad" moments where players spend resources and receive zero value due to bad luck, which can lead to non-games in a draft environment.
  - [elegant/minor] The mechanic is bloated by the inclusion of the counter if that counter does not interact with any other part of the game state. If the counter is meant to be used for something else, it hasn't been defined; if it isn't, it shouldn't be there.
### Reviewer 3 — verdict: REVISE
  - [unique/major] This is essentially a "filtering" version of Impetus or similar 'if X, do Y' effects, but more specifically, it functions as a highly conditional version of Impulse/Sleight of Hand. However, the core issue is that it provides repeatable card advantage and selection on a single permanent without any downside or cost to the Scanned counter itself. It feels like a weaker version of 'Improvise' or 'Convoke' in terms of resource usage, but instead of mana efficiency, it offers pure card advantage.
  - [interesting/major] The mechanic lacks tension. Because you only put the card in your hand *if* it is an artifact, there is no decision-making involved in the 'Scan' action itself—you either get a card or you don't. The Scanned counter is added regardless of whether the condition was met (based on the wording: "If it is an artifact... and put a Scanned counter"), which means players will spend mana to Scan, fail to find an artifact, and still receive a counter that does nothing. This creates a 'feel-bad' loop where you pay mana for a failed trigger.
  - [wording/minor] The reminder text is slightly ambiguous regarding the Scanned counter. "If it is an artifact, you may [A] and [B]." Does [B] (putting a counter) only happen if the 'if' condition is met? The current phrasing suggests the counter is part of the 'may' clause, meaning you only get a counter if you find an artifact. If that is the case, the mechanic is even more punishing/uninteresting as it becomes a 'hit or miss' engine with no consolation prize for missing.
  - [playable/minor] In Limited, this will lead to very 'clunky' gameplay. You are paying mana to look at one card. If you don't hit an artifact, you have essentially just spent mana to pass the turn or slightly filter your deck. Without a way to 'fail forward', players will find this mechanic frustrating in a draft environment.

## Your task

1. **Apply a consensus filter.** Act on issues that AT LEAST 2 members raised (or that a single member raised but is plainly, objectively correct — e.g. an example card that does not use the mechanic at all). DISCARD issues only one member raised that are matters of taste — those are likely false positives. Lone strong opinions are noise; agreement is signal.

2. **Revise the mechanic in place** to fix the consensus issues, keeping everything that already works. This is your primary tool — fixing is better than rejecting. Improve wording, sharpen the decision the mechanic creates, cut bloat, rewrite a broken example card. Respect the OFF-LIMITS list (never rename, never touch keyword_type, never optimise for theme). The `revised_mechanic` must be COMPLETE and well-formed against the same schema — every field present, exactly two example cards that use the mechanic at contrasting rarities.

   Fix the issue **in the text itself, not just in your notes** — a changelog claiming a fix you did not actually make is worse than no fix:
   - The `reminder_text` is the keyword's real definition. It must be self-contained and rules-complete: it states exactly what happens using only defined Magic terms and actions. NO undefined verbs or nouns (e.g. "switch its mode", "gain an Energon"), NO bracket placeholders (e.g. "[Cost]"). If the mechanic relies on a counter or token, the reminder text itself must say how that counter/token is created, what it is, and what it does — don't push the definition into the example cards and leave the reminder vague.
   - The `reminder_text` and BOTH example cards must be in **lockstep**: each example uses the keyword by name and behaves EXACTLY as the reminder text defines it. If you change the definition, change the examples to match, and vice-versa. If a reviewer flagged the wording, the revised wording must be genuinely different — don't re-emit the same flawed phrase.
   - **Never introduce a placeholder.** No `[cost]`, `[target]`, `[keyword]`, etc. Write the concrete cost/target, or put the cost on the keyword (`Crew 2`, `Equip {2}`).
   - **Never delete a definition the draft already had.** If the draft's reminder defined a token or counter (e.g. "a Scrap token is a colorless artifact with '{T}, Sacrifice this: Add {C}'"), KEEP that definition — stripping it leaves an undefined referent. Improve it; don't remove it.
   - **Plain text only** — no markdown, no asterisks, no emphasis formatting, in any field.
   - **Use correct Magic templating:**
     - Keyword ability with a cost → put the cost on the keyword (`Crew 2`, `Equip {2}`); the reminder defines the full ability. Not `{T}, pay [cost]`.
     - Activated ability → `[cost]: [effect]` (the cost may include `{T}`).
     - Optional ETB with a cost → `When this creature enters, you may [cost]. If you do, [effect].` — not `When this enters, [cost]: [effect]`.
     - Add a creature type → `is a Beast in addition to its other types` (or `is also a Beast`).
     - Toggle via a counter → `[cost]: Put a [name] counter on this creature. If it already has one, remove it instead.`

3. **Decide the verdict by re-reading the text you actually emitted, not your intentions.** Read your revised `reminder_text` and both example cards back as if you were a fresh reviewer. "OK" only if they are now genuinely sound, consistent, and rules-complete; "REVISE" if ANY consensus issue still stands in the text you wrote (it will get another round). When in doubt, REVISE. The council will independently re-review your revision, so an over-confident "OK" only wastes a round.

Submit with the `submit_mechanic_synthesis` tool:
- `synthesis`: one or two sentences on what the council agreed on.
- `consensus_issues`: the issues you acted on, each with its `agreement` count.
- `revised_mechanic`: the complete improved mechanic (or the original unchanged if the council found nothing real).
- `verdict`: OK or REVISE, judging the revised mechanic.
- `review_notes`: one or two sentences naming what you changed and why (empty string if you changed nothing).

consensus_issues.description: The Scanned counters provided no gameplay value or tension; they were 'dead' effects.
The mechanic was too unreliable in Limited because missing the artifact condition resulted in a complete loss of mana and tempo with no consolation prize.
The mechanic included unnecessary complexity (counters) that didn't serve a purpose.

input.review_notes: I redefined Scan to ensure the counter provides utility by allowing the permanent to tap for mana if it has a Scanned counter, and I adjusted the trigger so that players always get some value (scrying) even if they miss the artifact, preventing 'do-nothing' turns.

revised_mechanic.design_rationale: Scan provides a way to filter for artifacts while building up a resource that can be tapped for mana, creating a tension between using the ability for card advantage or saving mana for later utility.

example_cards.name: Scout Drone
example_cards.oracle_text: Scan {1}: Look at the top card of your library. If it is an artifact, you may put it into your hand and put a Scanned counter on Scout Drone. Otherwise, scry 1. {T}: Add {C} only if Scout Drone has a Scanned counter on it.

example_cards.name: Cybertronian Sensor Array
example_cards.oracle_text: Scan {2}: Look at the top card of your library. If it is an artifact, you may put it into your hand and put a Scanned counter on Cybertronian Sensor Array. Otherwise, scry 1. {T}: Add {C} only if Cybertronian Sensor Array has a Scanned counter on it.

revised_mechanic.name: Scan
revised_mechanic.reminder_text: Scan {cost}: Look at the top card of your library. If it is an artifact, you may put it into your hand and put a Scanned counter on this permanent. Otherwise, scry 1.

input.synthesis: The council agreed that the original mechanic was a 'dead' effect because the counters had no utility and the ability was too punishing when it failed to find an artifact. The revised version ensures the counters provide mana utility and provides a 'fail-forward' scry effect if no artifact is found.

cache_summary._note: No cache activity reported. This provider may not expose cache stats, or the prompt was below the cache threshold.
